title.emphasis: Introduction: The Duality of Digital Creation

section.para: Intellectual property (IP) embodies a fundamental tension between the internal act of creation and its external manifestation as a legally defined, economically valuable asset. In the digital age, the mechanisms for contracting this IP—Terms of Service (ToS), unilateral contracts, and licensing agreements—are designed to facilitate the frictionless transfer of rights. However, this system is built on a critical ambiguity: Is the creator selling a distinct asset (a "good") or providing a "service"?

The resolution of this ambiguity has profound consequences. If the transaction is a sale of goods, it is governed by commercial law. If it is a contract for services, it triggers labor laws, the presumption of employment, and potentially non-disclaimable fiduciary duties. As platforms increasingly rely on user-generated content (UGC), AI feedback (RLHF), and decentralized labor, the legal and economic structures governing IP acquisition are facing unprecedented scrutiny.
